metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = quantum wave scattering / forward diffraction / shadow-forming wave; CRIMSON = classical geometric shadow / hit-or-miss
metadata.note: No partial-wave phase-shift sum, no Legendre expansion, no optical-theorem proof.

beats.narration_text: Shoot particles at a hard sphere of radius a. Classically, only the ones aimed at the disk hit it — total target area is pi a-squared. Quantum mechanics: the area is 4 pi a-squared.
Even at high energy, when many partial waves contribute, the cross-section never drops to the classical value. It stays at twice the geometric area.
Why does a quantum sphere collect more particles than its geometric shadow? And why does the extra piece never go away?

props.headline: Why does a quantum sphere collect more particles than its geometric…

card.headline: Low energy: 4πa². High energy: 2πa². Why always more than πa²?
card.dek: The optical theorem: casting a shadow requires scattering

beats.narration_text: The classical picture misses what happens to the wave. A quantum particle is a wave that diffracts around the sphere and spreads into all solid angles — even where classically nothing should go.
At low energy only the s-wave matters. It scatters isotropically in all 4 pi steradians, producing 4 pi a-squared — four times the disk. Not an error: the wave has no preferred direction.
At high energy the sphere blocks the forward beam. But blocking requires destructive interference — the forward-scattered wave must cancel the incident wave to create a dark shadow downstream.
That forward-scattered wave carries probability. It contributes pi a-squared to the total cross-section on top of the pi a-squared from sideways scattering. The shadow counts.
This is the optical theorem: total cross-section equals 4 pi over k times the imaginary part of the forward scattering amplitude. It holds for any potential — even inelastic — because of unitarity.
Every absorbing or scattering object casts a shadow, and casting that shadow requires forward scattering that is always counted. Quantum objects can never look smaller than their geometric shadow.

Illustrative: hard sphere, radius a equals 2 angstroms. Low energy, k a equals 0.1: sigma equals 4 pi a-squared, 16 times ten to the minus 20 square meters. High energy, k a equals 10: sigma approaches 2 pi a-squared.
Low energy: isotropic diffraction gives 4 pi a-squared. High energy: shadow formation forces 2 pi a-squared. Classical pi a-squared is always wrong. The shadow is half the price of blocking.

props.headline: Low energy: isotropic diffraction gives 4 pi a-squared. High energy: shadow…

card.headline: σ_tot = (4π/k) Im[f(0)]
card.dek: Blocking the beam requires forward scattering. The shadow counts — quantum cross-sections always exceed πa².
